extracted_information: The provided web content details the trade-offs between monolithic and microservices architectures, including specific points on scalability, performance, maintenance, development complexity, and deployment considerations. It uses Netflix and Atlassian's migration experiences as case studies.

specifications.monolithic_architecture: A traditional model built as a single unified unit with one codebase, coupling all business concerns. Updates require updating the entire stack.
specifications.microservices_architecture: An architectural method relying on a series of smaller, independently deployable services, each with its own business logic and database. Updating, testing, deployment, and scaling occur within each service.

disadvantages: Scaling becomes a challenge as the application grows too large.

advantages: Flexible scaling: New instances of a service can be rapidly deployed to relieve pressure if it reaches load capacity.
Enables scaling teams and geographic locations more reliably by splitting along lines of service ownership (Atlassian's experience).
In a centralized codebase, one API can often perform the same function that numerous APIs perform with microservices (potentially simpler calls for intertwined functions).
Improved reliability, uptime, and performance (Atlassian's experience).
Simplified testing: Single, centralized unit allows faster end-to-end testing.
Easy debugging: All code in one place makes it easier to follow a request and find an issue.
disadvantages: Making a small change requires compiling and testing the entire platform.
Barrier to technology adoption: Changes in framework or language affect the entire application, making them expensive and time-consuming.
Lack of flexibility: Constrained by existing technologies.
Reliability: An error in any module could affect the entire application's availability.

advantages: Highly maintainable and testable: Teams can experiment with new features and roll back; easy to isolate and fix faults and bugs in individual services.
disadvantages: Debugging challenges: Each microservice has its own set of logs, making debugging more complicated. A single business process can run across multiple machines, further complicating debugging.
Lack of standardization: Without a common platform, leads to proliferation of languages, logging standards, and monitoring.
Lack of clear ownership: As more services are introduced, it becomes difficult to know available services and who to contact for support.

advantages: Fast development speed due to simplicity of having an application based on one codebase (early project stages).
Convenient early on for ease of code management, cognitive overhead.
disadvantages: Slower development speed: A large, monolithic application makes development more complex and slower.
Code conflicts become more frequent as more developers work on a singular codebase.
Increased risk of updates to one feature introducing bugs in an unrelated feature.

advantages: Agility: Promotes agile ways of working with small teams that deploy frequently.
Technology flexibility: Teams have freedom to select desired tools.
Happier teams: More autonomous, can build and deploy themselves without long waits.
disadvantages: Development sprawl: Adds more complexity compared to a monolith, with more services in more places created by multiple teams. If unmanaged, leads to slower development speed and poor operational performance.
Added organizational overhead: Teams need an additional level of communication and collaboration to coordinate updates and interfaces.
Does not reduce complexity, but makes it visible and more manageable by separating tasks.
Challenging to determine how different components relate, who owns what, or how to avoid interfering with dependent components.

advantages: Easy deployment: One executable file or directory makes deployment simpler.
disadvantages: A small change requires the redeployment of the entire monolith.

advantages: Independently deployable: Allows for fast and easy independent deployment of individual features.
Continuous deployment: Enables frequent and faster release cycles (Atlassian went from weekly updates to 2-3 times/day).
Each service can be developed, updated, deployed, and scaled without affecting other services.

atlassian_customer_migration.customer_migration_timeframe: Just over 10 months (with no service interruptions)

statistics.atlassian_deployment_frequency_increase: From once a week to two to three times a day.

temporal_info.atlassian_vertigo_project_duration: 2 years to complete transition to AWS

geographical_data.atlassian_development_centers_pre_vertig: Five different development centers around the world (constrained by centralized monolith).

references.title: Mike Tria, Head of Platform at Atlassian
